summary: In the introduction to 'Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach', Stuart Russell and Peter Norvig define AI and categorize its historical approaches. They advocate for the 'rational agent' approach—building systems that act to achieve their goals given their beliefs—as the most scientifically viable path. The text also outlines the foundational contributions of philosophy, mathematics, psychology, computer engineering, and linguistics to the development of AI.

principles.statement: AI is best viewed as the study and construction of rational agents.
principles.rationale: It is more general than the 'laws of thought' approach because correct inference is only one mechanism for achieving rationality. It is also more amenable to scientific development than human-based approaches because the standard of rationality is clearly defined.
principles.source_quote: The study of AI as rational agent design therefore has two advantages. First, it is more general than the “laws of thought” approach... Second, it is more amenable to scientific development than approaches based on human behavior or human thought

principles.statement: Perfect rationality is impossible in complicated environments.
principles.rationale: The computational demands of always calculating and doing the exact right thing are simply too high for complex real-world environments.
principles.source_quote: achieving perfect rationality—always doing the right thing—is not possible in complicated environments. The computational demands are just too high.

principles.statement: Understanding perfect decision making is the best starting point for AI research.
principles.rationale: Even though perfect rationality is impossible in practice, assuming it as a working hypothesis simplifies the problem and provides the necessary foundational setting before tackling limited rationality.
principles.source_quote: we will adopt the working hypothesis that understanding perfect decision making is a good place to start. It simplifies the problem and provides the appropriate setting for most of the foundational material in the field.

principles.statement: Intelligent behavior generation must be divided into tractable subproblems.
principles.rationale: Because of exponential growth in computational complexity, intractable problems cannot be solved in any reasonable time, making brute-force approaches to large problems impossible.
principles.source_quote: Therefore, one should strive to divide the overall problem of generating intelligent behavior into tractable subproblems rather than intractable ones.

frameworks.name: The Four Approaches to AI
frameworks.when_to_apply: When defining the goals, metrics, and historical methodologies of an artificial intelligence system.
steps: Determine if the system's goal is focused on thought processes/reasoning or on behavior/acting.
Determine if success is measured against human performance or against an ideal concept of intelligence (rationality).
Categorize the system into one of four quadrants: Systems that think like humans, Systems that think rationally, Systems that act like humans, or Systems that act rationally.
frameworks.source_quote: These definitions vary along two main dimensions. The ones on top are concerned with thought processes and reasoning, whereas the ones on the bottom address behavior. Also, the definitions on the left measure success in terms of human performance, whereas the ones on the right measure against an ideal concept of intelligence, which we will call rationality.

mental_models.name: Rationality vs. Human Behavior
mental_models.description: A lens for evaluating AI that separates 'doing what a human does' from 'doing the right thing'. Humans are adapted to a specific environment, make systematic errors, and are products of an unknown evolutionary process. Rationality provides a clearly defined, completely general mathematical standard of success.
mental_models.example: Noting that humans often make mistakes and are not all chess grandmasters, so an AI should strive to play the optimal move (rationality) rather than the move a typical human would make.

mental_models.name: The World as a Problem Instance
mental_models.description: Viewing the physical world through the lens of computational complexity theory. Because the world is so large, any attempt to compute optimal actions within it faces severe intractability and exponential growth in complexity.
mental_models.example: Applying NP-completeness theory to real-world AI tasks to recognize that the world is an 'extremely large problem instance' requiring subtlety and careful use of resources.

heuristics: A system is rational if it does the right thing.
Acting rationally means acting so as to achieve one's goals, given one's beliefs.

signature_quotes.text: AI addresses one of the ultimate puzzles. How is it possible for a slow, tiny brain, whether biological or electronic, to perceive, understand, predict, and manipulate a world far larger and more complicated than itself?
signature_quotes.context: Introducing the fundamental motivation for studying artificial intelligence.

signature_quotes.text: A system is rational if it does the right thing.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the concept of rationality in the context of AI goals.

signature_quotes.text: Put crudely, the world is an extremely large problem instance!
signature_quotes.context: Discussing computational intractability and NP-completeness in relation to generating intelligent behavior.

signature_quotes.text: achieving perfect rationality—always doing the right thing—is not possible in complicated environments. The computational demands are just too high.
signature_quotes.context: Acknowledging the limits of the rational agent approach in practice.

anti_patterns.description: Equating rationality entirely with correct logical inference (the 'laws of thought' approach).
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: There are often situations where there is no provably correct thing to do, yet action is required. Furthermore, some rational actions (like pulling a hand off a hot stove) are successful reflex actions that do not involve slow, careful deliberation.

anti_patterns.description: Attempting to achieve perfect rationality in complex, real-world environments.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The computational demands are too high. The time required to solve instances of the class grows at least exponentially with the size of the instances.
